description:
Step 3 of T-012's 4-step CSP roadmap. Depends on T-031 (Step 2 inline-handler refactor) not being a hard prereq — they're independent.

**Scope (inventoried 2026-05-15 with `grep -rl 'style="' app/templates/`):**
- 101 templates with inline `style="..."` attributes
- 3,296 total `style="` occurrences

**The right pattern:**
- Most uses are layout (`display:flex`, `gap:`, `width:`, color tokens like `var(--muted)`) that map cleanly to existing `b-*` utility classes in the design system OR to new utility classes worth adding.
- For one-offs (e.g., `style="width: {{ pct }}%"` for progress bars), convert to CSS custom properties: `style="--pct: {{ pct }}%"` + a static class `.bar-fill { width: var(--pct); }` — the nonce'd `<style>` block in base.html handles the static class, and the custom-property setter is still inline but contains no executable content (no CSP violation for inline style; the violation is on the `style=` attribute itself).

Wait — `style=` attribute IS what unsafe-inline allows. Removing unsafe-inline from style-src will block ALL `style=` attributes, regardless of content. Even `style="--pct: 80%"` would fail.

So either:
- Convert every dynamic value to JS-driven `element.style.setProperty()` after page load, or
- Accept `'unsafe-inline'` in style-src (drop only from script-src). Most apps do this — style is less of a XSS vector than script.

**Decision needed at start of this ticket:** drop style-src unsafe-inline entirely, or keep it (and only the script-src tightening lands in Step 4).

**Out of scope:** the inline event handlers (Step 2, separate ticket).

**Done when:** decision recorded, refactor (if any) executed, and browser devtools shows 0 CSP violations for inline styles across the major routes (or the decision documents why some remain acceptable).</description>
title: CSP Step 3: refactor 101 templates with inline style= attrs to utility classes (VVP-2026-007)
